content:
You are maintaining an interim design document for a Claude Code project.

Mode: create
Document slug: slug
Document path: /home/io/io-dev/io/.claude/docs/interim/slug.md

This is an INTERIM doc (status: interim) under .claude/docs/interim/. It will
eventually be merged into a canonical structure. For now, keep it focused on
describing CURRENT REALITY of the code.

Schema for interim docs (frontmatter at top of file, in YAML between --- lines):

  id: slug
  title: <human-readable title>
  status: interim
  created: <YYYY-MM-DD, set once on creation>
  last_updated: <YYYY-MM-DD, today's date when updating>
  last_synced_with_code: <YYYY-MM-DD, today's date>
  work_units:
    - <list of work-unit log filenames that contributed>
  implementation:
    - <list of code file paths this doc describes>
  related:
    - <informal list of related doc slugs>

Body sections (in this order):
  # <Title>
  <1-3 sentence summary>

  ## Purpose
  <what this feature/area does>

  ## Behavior
  <how it behaves, including caveats>

  ## Implementation Notes
  <where the code lives, key design decisions>

  ## Changelog
  <!-- IGNORE UNLESS SPECIFICALLY ASKED TO REVIEW DOCUMENT HISTORY -->
  ### YYYY-MM-DD
  <Description of changes made on this date. Most recent on top.>

YOUR JOB:
- This doc does NOT yet exist. Create it fresh based on the work unit log and summary.
- Set 'created' and 'last_updated' and 'last_synced_with_code' all to: 2026-05-28
- Populate work_units with: 2026-05-28_check-duplicate-doc-creation_083943
- Populate implementation with the file paths actually modified in this work unit
- Add an initial changelog entry dated 2026-05-28 describing the creation

OUTPUT RULES:
- Output ONLY the complete file content (frontmatter + body), nothing else
- Start with the opening --- of frontmatter
- Do NOT include preamble, explanation, or markdown code fences around the output
- The output replaces the entire file contents

# Work Unit Log: check-duplicate-doc-creation

Started: 2026-05-28T08:39:43+00:00
Session: ffa17917-b4de-4b76-bb68-25717ee917f0

---


## #PROMPT

_2026-05-28T08:44:02+00:00_

```
~initprompt~ check-duplicate-doc-creation

Read-only diagnostic. Do not modify anything. The interim-docs sanity check found that some single work units produced multiple near-duplicate docs (e.g. workstream-2b-token-gaps produced 4 claim-a docs; workstream-3c-promote-dialog produced 2). Determine whether update-docs.sh will keep creating duplicates on future multi-step work, now that the slug bugs are fixed.

Step one, read update-docs.sh's auto-detect logic — the part that decides whether an existing interim doc already covers the modified files (the AFFECTED_DOCS detection that greps interim docs' implementation: fields for the modified file paths), versus creating a new doc with a freshly-generated slug.

Step two, explain why one work unit produced multiple docs. Likely hypotheses to evaluate: (a) the slug-generation claude -p call returned a different slug on each invocation within related work, so the new-doc path ran repeatedly; (b) the auto-detect grep failed to match an existing doc because the modified file paths differed slightly between steps; (c) the corruption (now fixed) caused the match to fail. Determine which is the actual cause by examining the logic and the duplicate docs' implementation: fields.

Step three, determine if it is still live. Now that the slug/newline bugs are fixed, will a future multi-step work unit (e.g. Claim C's 5b-A through 5b-D, or the per-batch migration in workstream 6) that modifies overlapping files across steps correctly UPDATE one doc, or still CREATE multiple? 

Step four, if still live, recommend the minimal fix to the auto-detect matching so related steps converge on one doc. Do not apply it. If it was caused by the now-fixed corruption and will not recur, say so.

When done print: root cause of the duplicates, still-live yes/no, one-line fix recommendation if live. Stop.
```


## #ASSISTANT

Reading `update-docs.sh`'s auto-detect logic and the duplicate docs' implementation fields.


## #BASH
_Read implementation fields from duplicate docs_

```bash
python3 - << 'EOF'
import os, re

d = '.claude/docs/interim'

# Read the 4 claim-a docs - show their implementation: fields
claim_a_docs = [
    'claim-a-css-token-registry-gaps.md',
    'claim-a-token-registry-gaps-zindex.md',
    'claim-a-token-registry-gaps.md',
    'claim-a-token-registry-z-index-scale.md',
]

print("=== CLAIM-A DOCS — implementation: fields ===")
for fname in claim_a_docs:
    path = os.path.join(d, fname)
    with open(path, errors='replace') as f:
        content = f.read()
    m = re.search(r'^implementation:(.*?)(?=^\S)', content, re.MULTILINE | re.DOTALL)
    impl_text = m.group(0) if m else '(not found)'
    print(f'\n{fname}:')
    print(impl_text.rstrip())

print()
print("=== CLAIM-B DIALOG DOCS — implementation: fields ===")
dialog_docs = [
    'claim-b-dialog-promotion-migration.md',
    '2026-05-28-workstream-3c-promote-dialog.md',
]
for fname in dialog_docs:
    path = os.path.join(d, fname)
    with open(path, errors='replace') as f:
        content = f.read()
    m = re.search(r'^implementation:(.*?)(?=^\S)', content, re.MULTILINE | re.DOTALL)
    impl_text = m.group(0) if m else '(not found)'
    print(f'\n{fname}:')
    print(impl_text.rstrip())
EOF
```

## #PROMPT

_2026-05-28T08:56:13+00:00_

```
~initprompt~ fix-duplicate-doc-autodetect

Maintenance task on update-docs.sh. Fix the auto-detect logic so incremental work within one feature area converges on a single interim doc instead of spawning near-duplicates, WITHOUT over-merging genuinely distinct docs. A prior diagnostic established the root cause; read it for context if available in .claude/. Touch only .claude/ harness files.

Root cause (from diagnostic): the new-doc-vs-update decision is all-or-nothing on exact file paths. A new doc is created only when ZERO modified files appear in ANY existing doc's implementation: list. This correctly handles fresh features, but for incremental work across separate sessions in the same feature area, a later session whose FILES_MODIFIED are disjoint from the existing doc's implementation list fails the match and creates a duplicate. Factor A (corrupted-filename read -r truncation) is already resolved by the filename cleanup; this fixes the surviving Factor B.

Constraints on the fix — it must avoid two opposite failure modes:
- UNDER-merging (the current bug): related incremental work spawns duplicate docs.
- OVER-merging (the risk in naive fixes): distinct docs get collapsed. Specifically, matching on a shared slug prefix like "claim-b" or "workstream-3c" is NOT acceptable — claim-b-dialog-promotion and claim-b-shared-style-constants are both claim-b work but must stay separate docs. Do not key the match on slug prefix or on log-filename structure.

Design the fix:

Step one, evaluate options against both failure modes. Consider at least:
(a) Partial file-overlap with a threshold: take the update path if a meaningful share of FILES_MODIFIED (not just any single file, to avoid a shared common file like index.css collapsing everything) already appears in an existing doc's implementation list. Consider whether a single shared file like index.css or a shared util would cause over-merge, and how to avoid that (e.g. ignore very-high-frequency shared files when computing overlap, or require overlap on a module-specific file).
(b) An explicit work-unit-family tag: a lightweight mechanism where related sessions declare the same doc target (e.g. an optional ~docfresh:slug~ the user already has, or a family field). Evaluate whether the existing ~docfresh:slug~ tag already solves this if used deliberately.
(c) A hybrid: auto-detect by partial overlap on module-specific files, with ~docfresh:slug~ as the explicit override when the user knows several sessions belong to one doc.

Recommend the option that best avoids BOTH failure modes. Favor the least-magic option — explicit user control (docfresh) over clever inference where the inference is risky.

Step two, note the existing ~docfresh:slug~ capability. The harness already supports ~docfresh:slug~ (per stop.sh and user-prompt-submit.sh) which targets a specific doc by slug. Determine whether the simplest robust answer is: keep auto-detect conservative (accept that it errs toward new docs), and document that for multi-step work units the user should use ~docfresh:slug~ pointing at the same slug across sessions to converge them. This may be a documentation/convention fix rather than a code change. Report whether this is sufficient.

Step three, apply the chosen fix. If it is a code change to update-docs.sh, keep it minimal and show before/after. If a single shared file (index.css, a common util) could trigger over-merge under your chosen logic, explicitly guard against it. If the best answer is the ~docfresh:slug~ convention with no code change, document it clearly in WORKFLOW_NOTES.md instead.

Step four, verify. Construct test scenarios and simulate the decision:
- Two sessions, same feature, partially overlapping module-specific files → should UPDATE one doc.
- Two sessions, genuinely different features that happen to both touch index.css → should create TWO docs (no over-merge on the shared file).
- Two distinct claim-b features (dialog promotion vs style constants) → should stay TWO docs.
Report each scenario's expected vs actual decision under the fix.

Step five, document for the work ahead. Whatever the fix, write a short note in WORKFLOW_NOTES.md on how to keep Claim C (5b-A..5b-D) and Workstream 6 (per-batch migration) docs converged — whether that is automatic now, or requires using ~docfresh:slug~ with a consistent slug across the sub-sessions.

Constraints: only .claude/ harness files. No application source. Minimal change. Prefer explicit user control over risky inference.

When done print: chosen option with rationale, the change made (code before/after, or convention documented), the four verification scenarios' results, and the guidance for Claim C / Workstream 6 doc convergence. Stop.
```


## #ASSISTANT

Reading the diagnostic context and relevant harness files before designing the fix.


## #BASH
_Check for recent diagnostic output files_

```bash
ls .claude/docs/interim/*.md 2>/dev/null | head -5; ls .claude/reviews/ 2>/dev/null | tail -3; ls .claude/summaries/ 2>/dev/null | tail -3
```


## #ASSISTANT

Now I have the full picture. The key insight: `~wrapup~ ~docfresh:slug~` is already valid syntax but `classify_prompt_tags` classifies it as `wrapup` only (wrapup wins the priority check), so the slug is silently ignored and auto-detect runs instead. The fix is minimal: in the `wrapup` case in `stop.sh`, check for a docfresh slug in the same prompt and pass it through to `update-docs.sh` when present.

This avoids all inference risk — the user explicitly says which doc to target, exactly like bare `~docfresh:slug~`, but gets the full wrapup (summary + review + targeted doc update).


## #ASSISTANT

Now write the WORKFLOW_NOTES.md section:


## #ASSISTANT

Verify the changes look correct:


## #BASH
_Verify stop.sh edit_

```bash
grep -n "WRAPUP_DOC_SLUG\|docfresh\|extract_docfresh" /home/io/io-dev/io/.claude/hooks/stop.sh
```


## #BASH
_Shell syntax check on stop.sh_

```bash
bash -n /home/io/io-dev/io/.claude/hooks/stop.sh && echo "syntax OK"
```


## #ASSISTANT

Good. Now simulate the four verification scenarios:


## #BASH
_Simulate the four verification scenarios_

```bash
python3 - << 'EOF'
"""
Simulate the auto-detect + wrapup/docfresh decision logic for the four scenarios.
"""

def extract_docfresh_slug(prompt):
    import re
    m = re.search(r'\[docfresh:([a-z0-9-]+)\]', prompt)
    return m.group(1) if m else ''

def classify_tag(prompt):
    import re
    if re.search(r'\[wrapup\]', prompt): return 'wrapup'
    if re.search(r'\[deepreview\]', prompt): return 'deepreview'
    if re.search(r'\[review\]', prompt): return 'review'
    if re.search(r'\[docfresh:[a-z0-9-]+\]', prompt): return 'docfresh'
    return 'none'

def auto_detect(files_modified, existing_docs):
    """Returns list of matching doc names, or [] to trigger new-doc creation."""
    affected = set()
    for f in files_modified:
        for doc_name, impl_files in existing_docs.items():
            if f in impl_files:
                affected.add(doc_name)
    return sorted(affected)

def simulate_wrapup(prompt, files_modified, existing_docs, label):
    tag = classify_tag(prompt)
    slug = extract_docfresh_slug(prompt)
    
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"SCENARIO: {label}")
    print(f"  prompt tags:      [{tag}]{f' [docfresh:{slug}]' if slug else ''}")
    print(f"  files_modified:   {files_modified}")
    print(f"  existing_docs:    {list(existing_docs.keys())}")
    
    if tag == 'wrapup':
        if slug:
            print(f"  → wrapup+docfresh: targeted update of '{slug}.md'")
            if slug + '.md' in existing_docs:
                print(f"  DECISION: UPDATE '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode]")
            else:
                print(f"  DECISION: CREATE '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode, new doc]")
        else:
            affected = auto_detect(files_modified, existing_docs)
            if affected:
                print(f"  → wrapup auto-detect: matched {affected}")
                print(f"  DECISION: UPDATE {affected}  [auto-detect mode]")
            else:
                print(f"  → wrapup auto-detect: no match")
                print(f"  DECISION: CREATE new doc  [auto-detect mode]")
    elif tag == 'docfresh':
        print(f"  → docfresh: targeted update of '{slug}.md'")
        print(f"  DECISION: {'UPDATE' if slug+'.md' in existing_docs else 'CREATE'} '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode]")

print("VERIFICATION SCENARIOS\n")

# Scenario 1: Two sessions, same feature, partially overlapping module-specific files
# Session A created a doc from {Dialog.tsx, console/index.tsx, PaneConfigModal.tsx}
# Session B now touches {RestorePreviewModal.tsx, TabClosePrompt.tsx} — no overlap
docs_after_A = {
    'claim-c-canvas-migration.md': [
        'frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/index.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/PaneConfigModal.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/console/PaneConfigModal.tsx'],
    {},
    "1a: Session A — first session, no existing docs"
)

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]',
    ['frontend/src/pages/settings/RestorePreviewModal.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/TabClosePrompt.tsx'],
    docs_after_A,
    "1b: Session B — same feature, disjoint files, WITH [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]"
)

# Scenario 2: Two sessions, different features, both touch index.css → should be TWO docs
docs_after_tokenwork = {
    'claim-a-token-registry-gaps.md': [
        'frontend/src/index.css',
        'frontend/src/shared/components/CommandPalette.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/index.css', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/IographicExportDialog.tsx'],
    docs_after_tokenwork,
    "2: Different feature also touches index.css — auto-detect (WITHOUT docfresh)"
)

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-dialog-export]',
    ['frontend/src/index.css', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/IographicExportDialog.tsx'],
    docs_after_tokenwork,
    "2 (fixed): Different feature also touches index.css — WITH [docfresh:claim-c-dialog-export]"
)

# Scenario 3: Two distinct claim-b features stay separate
docs_claim_b_dialog = {
    'claim-b-dialog-promotion-migration.md': [
        'frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/index.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/shared/constants/buttons.ts', 'frontend/src/shared/constants/inputs.ts'],
    docs_claim_b_dialog,
    "3: Distinct claim-b style-constants work, no file overlap with dialog doc"
)

# Scenario 4 (bonus): wrapup with docfresh on first session (slug doesn't exist yet)
simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:workstream-6-batch-migration]',
    ['frontend/src/pages/settings/Users.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/settings/Roles.tsx'],
    {},
    "4: First session ever, user supplies explicit slug upfront"
)

EOF
```

Whatever the fix, write a short note in WORKFLOW_NOTES.md on how to keep Claim C (5b-A..5b-D) and Workstream 6 (per-batch migration) docs converged — whether that is automatic now, or requires using ~docfresh:slug~ with a consistent slug across the sub-sessions.

Constraints: only .claude/ harness files. No application source. Minimal change. Prefer explicit user control over risky inference.

When done print: chosen option with rationale, the change made (code before/after, or convention documented), the four verification scenarios' results, and the guidance for Claim C / Workstream 6 doc convergence. Stop.
```


## #ASSISTANT

Reading the diagnostic context and relevant harness files before designing the fix.


## #BASH
_Check for recent diagnostic output files_

```bash
ls .claude/docs/interim/*.md 2>/dev/null | head -5; ls .claude/reviews/ 2>/dev/null | tail -3; ls .claude/summaries/ 2>/dev/null | tail -3
```


## #ASSISTANT

Now I have the full picture. The key insight: `~wrapup~ ~docfresh:slug~` is already valid syntax but `classify_prompt_tags` classifies it as `wrapup` only (wrapup wins the priority check), so the slug is silently ignored and auto-detect runs instead. The fix is minimal: in the `wrapup` case in `stop.sh`, check for a docfresh slug in the same prompt and pass it through to `update-docs.sh` when present.

This avoids all inference risk — the user explicitly says which doc to target, exactly like bare `~docfresh:slug~`, but gets the full wrapup (summary + review + targeted doc update).


## #ASSISTANT

Now write the WORKFLOW_NOTES.md section:


## #ASSISTANT

Verify the changes look correct:


## #BASH
_Verify stop.sh edit_

```bash
grep -n "WRAPUP_DOC_SLUG\|docfresh\|extract_docfresh" /home/io/io-dev/io/.claude/hooks/stop.sh
```


## #BASH
_Shell syntax check on stop.sh_

```bash
bash -n /home/io/io-dev/io/.claude/hooks/stop.sh && echo "syntax OK"
```


## #ASSISTANT

Good. Now simulate the four verification scenarios:


## #BASH
_Simulate the four verification scenarios_

```bash
python3 - << 'EOF'
"""
Simulate the auto-detect + wrapup/docfresh decision logic for the four scenarios.
"""

def extract_docfresh_slug(prompt):
    import re
    m = re.search(r'\[docfresh:([a-z0-9-]+)\]', prompt)
    return m.group(1) if m else ''

def classify_tag(prompt):
    import re
    if re.search(r'\[wrapup\]', prompt): return 'wrapup'
    if re.search(r'\[deepreview\]', prompt): return 'deepreview'
    if re.search(r'\[review\]', prompt): return 'review'
    if re.search(r'\[docfresh:[a-z0-9-]+\]', prompt): return 'docfresh'
    return 'none'

def auto_detect(files_modified, existing_docs):
    """Returns list of matching doc names, or [] to trigger new-doc creation."""
    affected = set()
    for f in files_modified:
        for doc_name, impl_files in existing_docs.items():
            if f in impl_files:
                affected.add(doc_name)
    return sorted(affected)

def simulate_wrapup(prompt, files_modified, existing_docs, label):
    tag = classify_tag(prompt)
    slug = extract_docfresh_slug(prompt)
    
    print(f"\n{'='*60}")
    print(f"SCENARIO: {label}")
    print(f"  prompt tags:      [{tag}]{f' [docfresh:{slug}]' if slug else ''}")
    print(f"  files_modified:   {files_modified}")
    print(f"  existing_docs:    {list(existing_docs.keys())}")
    
    if tag == 'wrapup':
        if slug:
            print(f"  → wrapup+docfresh: targeted update of '{slug}.md'")
            if slug + '.md' in existing_docs:
                print(f"  DECISION: UPDATE '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode]")
            else:
                print(f"  DECISION: CREATE '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode, new doc]")
        else:
            affected = auto_detect(files_modified, existing_docs)
            if affected:
                print(f"  → wrapup auto-detect: matched {affected}")
                print(f"  DECISION: UPDATE {affected}  [auto-detect mode]")
            else:
                print(f"  → wrapup auto-detect: no match")
                print(f"  DECISION: CREATE new doc  [auto-detect mode]")
    elif tag == 'docfresh':
        print(f"  → docfresh: targeted update of '{slug}.md'")
        print(f"  DECISION: {'UPDATE' if slug+'.md' in existing_docs else 'CREATE'} '{slug}.md'  [targeted mode]")

print("VERIFICATION SCENARIOS\n")

# Scenario 1: Two sessions, same feature, partially overlapping module-specific files
# Session A created a doc from {Dialog.tsx, console/index.tsx, PaneConfigModal.tsx}
# Session B now touches {RestorePreviewModal.tsx, TabClosePrompt.tsx} — no overlap
docs_after_A = {
    'claim-c-canvas-migration.md': [
        'frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/index.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/PaneConfigModal.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/console/PaneConfigModal.tsx'],
    {},
    "1a: Session A — first session, no existing docs"
)

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]',
    ['frontend/src/pages/settings/RestorePreviewModal.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/TabClosePrompt.tsx'],
    docs_after_A,
    "1b: Session B — same feature, disjoint files, WITH [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]"
)

# Scenario 2: Two sessions, different features, both touch index.css → should be TWO docs
docs_after_tokenwork = {
    'claim-a-token-registry-gaps.md': [
        'frontend/src/index.css',
        'frontend/src/shared/components/CommandPalette.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/index.css', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/IographicExportDialog.tsx'],
    docs_after_tokenwork,
    "2: Different feature also touches index.css — auto-detect (WITHOUT docfresh)"
)

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-dialog-export]',
    ['frontend/src/index.css', 'frontend/src/pages/designer/components/IographicExportDialog.tsx'],
    docs_after_tokenwork,
    "2 (fixed): Different feature also touches index.css — WITH [docfresh:claim-c-dialog-export]"
)

# Scenario 3: Two distinct claim-b features stay separate
docs_claim_b_dialog = {
    'claim-b-dialog-promotion-migration.md': [
        'frontend/src/shared/components/Dialog.tsx',
        'frontend/src/pages/console/index.tsx',
    ]
}

simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup]',
    ['frontend/src/shared/constants/buttons.ts', 'frontend/src/shared/constants/inputs.ts'],
    docs_claim_b_dialog,
    "3: Distinct claim-b style-constants work, no file overlap with dialog doc"
)

# Scenario 4 (bonus): wrapup with docfresh on first session (slug doesn't exist yet)
simulate_wrapup(
    '[wrapup] [docfresh:workstream-6-batch-migration]',
    ['frontend/src/pages/settings/Users.tsx', 'frontend/src/pages/settings/Roles.tsx'],
    {},
    "4: First session ever, user supplies explicit slug upfront"
)

EOF
```

attachment.content:
- audit: Verify a spec unit's implementation against its authoritative spec. Reads the manifest, reads spec files, walks target code, catalogs every gap, and writes task files for each finding.
- spec: Read the implementation spec for a given I/O module. Usage: /spec <module>. Modules: console, process, designer, graphics, display-elements, shapes, opc
- design-qa: Inventory all implementations of a cross-cutting feature across the codebase, surface inconsistencies, ask targeted design questions, and write the answers as an authoritative decision file. Run this BEFORE /audit on any feature marked ⚠️ NOT SPECCED in the manifest.
- spec-index: Print a quick-reference index of every audit unit — unit ID, canonical name, wave, and current task status. Use this to look up the correct unit ID or module name before running spec-scout or feature-agent.
- agent-guide: Print a reference guide for all custom agents in this project — what each one does, when to use it, how to launch it, and usage examples.
- wrapup: Close the current work unit (summary + review + doc updates)
- review: Run a verification review of work done since the last [initprompt]
- deepreview: Run a deep verification review including full file contents
- summarize: Generate an LLM-based summary of the current work unit
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# Workflow Suite — Integration Notes
2	
3	## lib-common.sh customizations
4	
5	- **claude_p_with_timeout uses stdin via temp file**: prompt is written to a mktemp file and piped in (`< tmpfile`) rather than passed as a CLI arg. Avoids ARG_MAX limit when log content is embedded in the prompt (can be hundreds of KB). Note: `--bare` was considered to prevent hook recursion but skips keychain auth, breaking Max subscription OAuth — don't add it. Lock + tag sanitization handle recursion instead.
6	
7	- **workflow-action.lock**: `stop.sh` creates `.claude/state/workflow-action.lock` before spawning any background action (review/wrapup/deepreview/docfresh) and removes it when the subshell exits. Sub-session Stop hooks check for this lock and skip if held, preventing recursive action triggers caused by embedded tags in log content passed to `claude -p`. Stale locks (>15 min) are cleaned by `user-prompt-submit.sh` on every real user prompt.
8	
9	- `WORKFLOW_IDLE_ROTATE_SECONDS`: left at default 14400 (4 hours)
10	- **Content-check before idle rotation**: modified `get_current_log_path()` to skip rotation when the current log has no `## #` section markers (i.e. is header-only). Prevents accumulating empty log stubs when a session is idle across multiple 4-hour windows.
11	
12	- **claude_p_with_timeout CWD fix (2026-05-28)**: Added project-root derivation before every `claude -p` invocation. The function now: (1) uses `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` if set and contains `.claude/`, otherwise (2) derives the root from `BASH_SOURCE[0]` (lib-common.sh is at `.claude/hooks/scripts/`, so `../../../` is the repo root). The `claude -p` call runs inside `$(cd "$project_dir" && ...)` so the sub-session always starts from the repo root regardless of the inherited shell CWD. This fixed hook paths inside sub-sessions going to nonexistent `frontend/.claude/hooks/...` paths.
13	
14	## Parent hook (`${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}`) resolution
15	
16	Hooks are registered in `~/.claude/settings.json` as `"${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/<script>.sh"`. The harness expands `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` at invocation time from the session's project root (determined by walking up from the launch CWD to find `.git`/`.claude/`). For the main session this is always correct.
17	
18	All individual hook scripts (`session-end.sh`, `stop.sh`, `user-prompt-submit.sh`) already have a self-correcting fallback (`if [ -z "${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-}" ]; then CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"`). This is belt-and-suspenders for the main session; it cannot help if the harness expands a wrong path and the script file is not found.
19	
20	**The only real failure path** was `claude -p` sub-sessions inheriting a drifted shell CWD — fixed by the `claude_p_with_timeout` change above. **Convention**: always launch Claude Code (`claude` CLI) from the repo root. No hook-registration change (option c) or settings-level pinning (option b) is needed.
21	
22	## Multi-session doc convergence — [wrapup] [docfresh:slug]
23	
24	### Root cause of near-duplicate docs
25	
26	`update-docs.sh` auto-detect creates a new doc when ZERO of the session's modified files appear in any existing doc's `implementation:` list. For the first session on a feature this is correct. For follow-on sessions that touch *different but related* files (e.g. Claim C sub-sessions 5b-A through 5b-D each migrating a different batch of files), the new files aren't in any existing doc's impl list → auto-detect fires → duplicate created.
27	
28	### The fix: combine [wrapup] with [docfresh:slug]
29	
30	`stop.sh` now checks for a `[docfresh:slug]` tag in the same prompt as `[wrapup]`. When both are present, the wrapup runs in full (summary + review) but `update-docs.sh` is called in targeted mode with the explicit slug instead of auto-detect. The slug is created if it doesn't exist yet.
31	
32	**Session A (first session for a feature):**
33	```
34	[wrapup]
35	```
36	Auto-detect runs. Creates a new doc (e.g. `claim-c-canvas-migration.md`) with slug derived from claude -p.
37	
38	**Sessions B, C, D (follow-on sessions for the same feature):**
39	```
40	[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]
41	```
42	Full wrapup (summary + shallow review), doc update targets `claim-c-canvas-migration.md` directly.
43	
44	`[docfresh:slug]` alone (without `[wrapup]`) also works — doc update only, no summary or review.
45	
46	### Why not auto-infer the target doc?
47	
48	Matching on shared file paths is risky: `frontend/src/index.css` or a shared utility will appear in many unrelated docs' implementation lists, causing over-merge. Matching on slug prefix (e.g. "claim-c") is explicitly rejected because distinct features share prefixes. Explicit user intent via `[docfresh:slug]` is zero-risk and requires no heuristics.
49	
50	### Convention for Claim C (5b-A..5b-D) and Workstream 6 (per-batch migration)
51	
52	For any multi-session workstream where sessions are logically one doc:
53	
54	1. Pick a slug at the start of the workstream (e.g. `claim-c-canvas-widget-migration`).
55	2. Session A: `[wrapup]` — auto-detect creates the doc. Note the slug it used.
56	3. Sessions B onward: `[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-widget-migration]` — converges on the same doc.
57	
58	If you forget the slug from session A, find it with:
59	```bash
60	ls .claude/docs/interim/ | grep claim-c
61	```
62	
63	If auto-detect on session A creates a doc with an ugly/wrong slug, rename it manually and use the clean slug from session B onward.
64	
65	## Known minor quirk: update-docs.sh frontmatter validation
66	
67	`update-docs.sh` checks that the output of `claude -p` starts with `---` (YAML frontmatter). When `claude -p` emits a tool-status line (e.g. `"File created at the specified path."`) before the frontmatter, the check fails and the proposal fallback triggers. This caused a spurious `.update-proposal-*` file during the FP-1 wrapup. Future hardening: strip all leading non-`---` lines before the frontmatter check. Low priority; does not affect correctness of the generated doc.
68	
69	## SECRET_PATTERNS / DANGEROUS_PATTERNS
70	
71	No changes from defaults. Project doesn't use Stripe/AWS/etc. in code; existing patterns are appropriate.
72	
73	## commands/*.md
74	
75	All commands updated to use `${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/scripts/...` instead of the bundle's `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/...` placeholder paths.
76

file.content:
# Workflow Suite — Integration Notes

## lib-common.sh customizations

- **claude_p_with_timeout uses stdin via temp file**: prompt is written to a mktemp file and piped in (`< tmpfile`) rather than passed as a CLI arg. Avoids ARG_MAX limit when log content is embedded in the prompt (can be hundreds of KB). Note: `--bare` was considered to prevent hook recursion but skips keychain auth, breaking Max subscription OAuth — don't add it. Lock + tag sanitization handle recursion instead.

- **workflow-action.lock**: `stop.sh` creates `.claude/state/workflow-action.lock` before spawning any background action (review/wrapup/deepreview/docfresh) and removes it when the subshell exits. Sub-session Stop hooks check for this lock and skip if held, preventing recursive action triggers caused by embedded tags in log content passed to `claude -p`. Stale locks (>15 min) are cleaned by `user-prompt-submit.sh` on every real user prompt.

- `WORKFLOW_IDLE_ROTATE_SECONDS`: left at default 14400 (4 hours)
- **Content-check before idle rotation**: modified `get_current_log_path()` to skip rotation when the current log has no `## #` section markers (i.e. is header-only). Prevents accumulating empty log stubs when a session is idle across multiple 4-hour windows.

- **claude_p_with_timeout CWD fix (2026-05-28)**: Added project-root derivation before every `claude -p` invocation. The function now: (1) uses `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` if set and contains `.claude/`, otherwise (2) derives the root from `BASH_SOURCE[0]` (lib-common.sh is at `.claude/hooks/scripts/`, so `../../../` is the repo root). The `claude -p` call runs inside `$(cd "$project_dir" && ...)` so the sub-session always starts from the repo root regardless of the inherited shell CWD. This fixed hook paths inside sub-sessions going to nonexistent `frontend/.claude/hooks/...` paths.

## Parent hook (`${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}`) resolution

Hooks are registered in `~/.claude/settings.json` as `"${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/<script>.sh"`. The harness expands `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` at invocation time from the session's project root (determined by walking up from the launch CWD to find `.git`/`.claude/`). For the main session this is always correct.

All individual hook scripts (`session-end.sh`, `stop.sh`, `user-prompt-submit.sh`) already have a self-correcting fallback (`if [ -z "${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-}" ]; then CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"`). This is belt-and-suspenders for the main session; it cannot help if the harness expands a wrong path and the script file is not found.

**The only real failure path** was `claude -p` sub-sessions inheriting a drifted shell CWD — fixed by the `claude_p_with_timeout` change above. **Convention**: always launch Claude Code (`claude` CLI) from the repo root. No hook-registration change (option c) or settings-level pinning (option b) is needed.

## Multi-session doc convergence — [wrapup] [docfresh:slug]

### Root cause of near-duplicate docs

`update-docs.sh` auto-detect creates a new doc when ZERO of the session's modified files appear in any existing doc's `implementation:` list. For the first session on a feature this is correct. For follow-on sessions that touch *different but related* files (e.g. Claim C sub-sessions 5b-A through 5b-D each migrating a different batch of files), the new files aren't in any existing doc's impl list → auto-detect fires → duplicate created.

### The fix: combine [wrapup] with [docfresh:slug]

`stop.sh` now checks for a `[docfresh:slug]` tag in the same prompt as `[wrapup]`. When both are present, the wrapup runs in full (summary + review) but `update-docs.sh` is called in targeted mode with the explicit slug instead of auto-detect. The slug is created if it doesn't exist yet.

**Session A (first session for a feature):**
```
[wrapup]
```
Auto-detect runs. Creates a new doc (e.g. `claim-c-canvas-migration.md`) with slug derived from claude -p.

**Sessions B, C, D (follow-on sessions for the same feature):**
```
[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-migration]
```
Full wrapup (summary + shallow review), doc update targets `claim-c-canvas-migration.md` directly.

`[docfresh:slug]` alone (without `[wrapup]`) also works — doc update only, no summary or review.

### Why not auto-infer the target doc?

Matching on shared file paths is risky: `frontend/src/index.css` or a shared utility will appear in many unrelated docs' implementation lists, causing over-merge. Matching on slug prefix (e.g. "claim-c") is explicitly rejected because distinct features share prefixes. Explicit user intent via `[docfresh:slug]` is zero-risk and requires no heuristics.

### Convention for Claim C (5b-A..5b-D) and Workstream 6 (per-batch migration)

For any multi-session workstream where sessions are logically one doc:

1. Pick a slug at the start of the workstream (e.g. `claim-c-canvas-widget-migration`).
2. Session A: `[wrapup]` — auto-detect creates the doc. Note the slug it used.
3. Sessions B onward: `[wrapup] [docfresh:claim-c-canvas-widget-migration]` — converges on the same doc.

If you forget the slug from session A, find it with:
```bash
ls .claude/docs/interim/ | grep claim-c
```

If auto-detect on session A creates a doc with an ugly/wrong slug, rename it manually and use the clean slug from session B onward.

## Known minor quirk: update-docs.sh frontmatter validation

`update-docs.sh` checks that the output of `claude -p` starts with `---` (YAML frontmatter). When `claude -p` emits a tool-status line (e.g. `"File created at the specified path."`) before the frontmatter, the check fails and the proposal fallback triggers. This caused a spurious `.update-proposal-*` file during the FP-1 wrapup. Future hardening: strip all leading non-`---` lines before the frontmatter check. Low priority; does not affect correctness of the generated doc.

## SECRET_PATTERNS / DANGEROUS_PATTERNS

No changes from defaults. Project doesn't use Stripe/AWS/etc. in code; existing patterns are appropriate.

## commands/*.md

All commands updated to use `${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/scripts/...` instead of the bundle's `~/.claude/hooks/scripts/...` placeholder paths.

content.content:
1	#!/bin/bash
2	# stop.sh — Stop hook
3	#
4	# Fires when Claude finishes responding to a turn. Responsibilities:
5	#   1. Extract the turn's content from the transcript into the work-unit log
6	#   2. If the prompt for this turn had [wrapup], trigger wrap-up sequence
7	#   3. If [review], trigger review only
8	#   4. If [deepreview], trigger deepreview
9	#   5. If [docfresh:slug], trigger targeted doc refresh
10	#   6. Clean up turn state
11	#
12	# Input (from stdin, JSON):
13	#   - session_id, transcript_path, hook_event_name, stop_hook_active
14	#
15	# Output:
16	#   - exit 0 always; we never block Claude from finishing
17	
18	set -uo pipefail
19	
20	# Auto-detect project dir from script location if not set in environment
21	if [ -z "${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-}" ]; then
22	    CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"
23	    export CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR
24	fi
25	
26	HOOK_NAME="stop"
27	export HOOK_NAME
28	
29	LIB_PATH="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/scripts/lib-common.sh"
30	if [ ! -f "$LIB_PATH" ]; then
31	    echo "WORKFLOW HOOK ERROR: lib-common.sh not found at $LIB_PATH" >&2
32	    exit 0
33	fi
34	# shellcheck source=/dev/null
35	source "$LIB_PATH"
36	
37	INPUT=$(cat)
38	
39	SESSION_ID=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.session_id // ""')
40	TRANSCRIPT_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.transcript_path // ""')
41	STOP_HOOK_ACTIVE=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.stop_hook_active // false')
42	# v2.1.143+: Stop hook input includes the final assistant turn text directly.
43	# Used as a fallback in extract-turn.sh when the transcript slice is empty.
44	LAST_ASSISTANT_MSG=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.last_assistant_message // ""')
45	
46	export WORKFLOW_SESSION_ID="$SESSION_ID"
47	ensure_workflow_dirs
48	
49	# Lock file used to prevent recursive action triggers from claude -p sub-sessions.
50	# When a review/wrapup/etc. spawns claude -p, that sub-session's Stop hook would
51	# re-detect tags embedded in the log content and trigger another action. The lock
52	# blocks that. Stale locks (older than 15 min) are cleaned up by user-prompt-submit.sh.
53	WORKFLOW_LOCK="${WORKFLOW_STATE_DIR}/workflow-action.lock"
54	
55	if [ "$STOP_HOOK_ACTIVE" = "true" ]; then
56	    hook_debug "stop_hook_active=true, skipping to avoid loop"
57	    exit 0
58	fi
59	
60	# Read turn state recorded by UserPromptSubmit

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
# stop.sh — Stop hook
#
# Fires when Claude finishes responding to a turn. Responsibilities:
#   1. Extract the turn's content from the transcript into the work-unit log
#   2. If the prompt for this turn had [wrapup], trigger wrap-up sequence
#   3. If [review], trigger review only
#   4. If [deepreview], trigger deepreview
#   5. If [docfresh:slug], trigger targeted doc refresh
#   6. Clean up turn state
#
# Input (from stdin, JSON):
#   - session_id, transcript_path, hook_event_name, stop_hook_active
#
# Output:
#   - exit 0 always; we never block Claude from finishing

set -uo pipefail

# Auto-detect project dir from script location if not set in environment
if [ -z "${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-}" ]; then
    CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"
    export CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR
fi

HOOK_NAME="stop"
export HOOK_NAME

LIB_PATH="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR}/.claude/hooks/scripts/lib-common.sh"
if [ ! -f "$LIB_PATH" ]; then
    echo "WORKFLOW HOOK ERROR: lib-common.sh not found at $LIB_PATH" >&2
    exit 0
fi
# shellcheck source=/dev/null
source "$LIB_PATH"

INPUT=$(cat)

SESSION_ID=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.session_id // ""')
TRANSCRIPT_PATH=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.transcript_path // ""')
STOP_HOOK_ACTIVE=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.stop_hook_active // false')
# v2.1.143+: Stop hook input includes the final assistant turn text directly.
# Used as a fallback in extract-turn.sh when the transcript slice is empty.
LAST_ASSISTANT_MSG=$(echo "$INPUT" | jq -r '.last_assistant_message // ""')

export WORKFLOW_SESSION_ID="$SESSION_ID"
ensure_workflow_dirs

# Lock file used to prevent recursive action triggers from claude -p sub-sessions.
# When a review/wrapup/etc. spawns claude -p, that sub-session's Stop hook would
# re-detect tags embedded in the log content and trigger another action. The lock
# blocks that. Stale locks (older than 15 min) are cleaned up by user-prompt-submit.sh.
WORKFLOW_LOCK="${WORKFLOW_STATE_DIR}/workflow-action.lock"

if [ "$STOP_HOOK_ACTIVE" = "true" ]; then
    hook_debug "stop_hook_active=true, skipping to avoid loop"
    exit 0
fi

# Read turn state recorded by UserPromptSubmit

# Interim Doc Auto-Detect Fix — Multi-Session Doc Convergence

Diagnosed and fixed a harness bug where a single multi-step feature workstream produced near-duplicate interim docs instead of updating one. The fix adds `[wrapup] [docfresh:slug]` combined-tag support to `stop.sh` and documents the convergence convention in `WORKFLOW_NOTES.md`.

## Purpose

The `update-docs.sh` auto-detect logic decides whether to update an existing interim doc or create a new one by checking whether any of the session's modified files appear in an existing doc's `implementation:` list. This works for first sessions on a feature but fails for follow-on sessions that touch different (but related) files — those sessions see zero overlap and create a new doc instead of updating the existing one.

## Behavior

**Before the fix:** Each session in a multi-step workstream (e.g. Claim C sub-sessions 5b-A through 5b-D, each migrating a different batch of files) would create its own doc because the new files had no entry in any prior doc's `implementation:` list. Result: 4–5 near-duplicate docs for one logical feature.

**After the fix:** `stop.sh` detects when both `[wrapup]` and `[docfresh:slug]` appear in the same prompt. When both are present, the full wrapup runs (summary + review) but `update-docs.sh` is called in targeted mode with the explicit slug, bypassing auto-detect entirely. The slug is created if it doesn't exist yet.

**Why not auto-infer?** Matching on shared files is risky — `index.css` or a shared utility can appear in many unrelated docs' implementation lists, causing over-merge across genuinely distinct features. Explicit `[docfresh:slug]` is zero-risk.

**Convention for multi-session workstreams:**
- Session A: `[wrapup]` — auto-detect creates the doc; note the slug it produces.
- Sessions B onward: `[wrapup] [docfresh:<slug>]` — full wrapup, targets the same doc.
- `[docfresh:slug]` alone (without `[wrapup]`) also works for doc-only updates with no summary/review.

## Implementation Notes

**`stop.sh`** (`WRAPUP_DOC_SLUG` extraction): after classifying the prompt tag as `wrapup`, the script now also checks for a `[docfresh:slug]` pattern in the same prompt text. If found, the slug is exported as `WRAPUP_DOC_SLUG` and passed to `update-docs.sh`'s `--slug` flag, switching it to targeted mode.

**`WORKFLOW_NOTES.md`** documents the root cause analysis, the chosen fix rationale (explicit user intent over heuristic inference), and the convention for Claim C (5b-A..5b-D) and Workstream 6 (per-batch migration) to keep their docs converged.

Known minor issue noted in `WORKFLOW_NOTES.md`: `update-docs.sh` frontmatter validation fails when `claude -p` emits a tool-status line before the `---` opening. A spurious `.update-proposal-*` file results; correctness is not affected. Low-priority hardening candidate.

## Changelog

<!-- IGNORE UNLESS SPECIFICALLY ASKED TO REVIEW DOCUMENT HISTORY -->
### 2026-05-28
Created. Documents root-cause diagnosis of near-duplicate interim docs and the `[wrapup] [docfresh:slug]` combined-tag fix applied to `stop.sh` and `WORKFLOW_NOTES.md`.

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.
